title: Constrained integrability and anyonic chains
authors: Matthew Blakeney, Luke Corcoran, Marius de Leeuw
abstract: We review the notion of Yang-Baxter integrability for spin chains that have Hilbert spaces with constraints, such as a Rydberg blockade. We focus on anyonic chains, whose constraints arise from the fusion rules of the fusion categories on which they are based. We discuss the emergence of Temperley-Lieb algebras and present a new result on which types of anyonic chains exhibit them. We then give an overview of known results for integrable anyonic chains and extend them to several fusion categories up to rank $7$. Using a modification of the boost operator formalism, we find several new integrable anyonic chains and discuss some of their properties. These include spin-$\frac32$ models for $\mathfrak{su}(2)_k$ fusion categories, anyonic chains based on the Tambara-Yamagami fusion categories TY$(\mathbb{Z}_n)$, and product fusion categories Fib$\times$Fib and Fib$\times$Ising. We review recent results for spin chains based on the Haagerup-Izumi fusion category HI$(\mathbb{Z}_3)$, and present preliminary numerics for a HI$(\mathbb{Z}_5)$ model.
summary: This work systematically analyzes the concept of integrability in anyonic chains, which are quantum models built upon the abstract structure of fusion categories. By utilizing the constrained boost operator formalism, the authors classify numerous new integrable models, providing a powerful mathematical framework for studying constrained quantum systems beyond simple spin chains.
main_problem: The paper reviews and classifies Yang-Baxter integrability for spin chains whose Hilbert spaces are constrained by mathematical structures, specifically focusing on anyonic chains.
main_result: The authors classify new integrable anyonic chains for various fusion categories, including those based on $\mathfrak{su}(2)_k$, Tambara-Yamagami categories, and product categories.
method: The study employs the constrained boost operator formalism and relies heavily on the mathematical framework of fusion categories, which requires solving the pentagon equations for consistency.
model_or_system: The primary model is the anyonic chain, which generalizes spin chains by using fusion rules from a fusion category $C$ to define local projection operators $P_a^{b,i}$ that form the Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Integrability, central charge ($c$), entanglement entropy, and the existence of Temperley-Lieb algebra representations.
important_parameters: The rank of the fusion category (up to 7), the specific fusion category (e.g., $	ext{Fib} 	imes 	ext{Fib}$), and the coupling constants ($\alpha_i,  eta_i$) in the Hamiltonian.
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be periodic, and the mathematical structure is assumed to be governed by a consistent fusion category satisfying the pentagon equations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate fusion diagrams, the measurement process for three particles, and plots showing entanglement entropy and gap scaling with chain length.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first defining the mathematical machinery (fusion rings, fusion categories, pentagon equations), then introducing the Hamiltonian via local projection operators, and finally applying the constrained boost formalism to classify and derive specific integrable models for various known fusion categories.
why_interesting: The deep connection between topological order (anyons/fusion categories) and exactly solvable quantum models (integrability) provides a powerful theoretical tool for understanding critical phenomena in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
